items.q: What is the best way to draw what you see accurately?
items.correct: Look closely and draw the shapes, proportions and tones you actually observe.
items.misconception: Draw what you know the object looks like from memory.
items.why: Students draw symbolic versions from memory (e.g. eyes placed too high on a face) instead of observing actual proportions and tones.

items.q: Is using an eraser a sign of bad drawing?
items.correct: No — erasers can lift highlights and refine marks, working as a drawing tool.
items.misconception: Yes — good artists never need to erase.
items.random_wrong: Yes — erasers permanently damage all paper.
items.why: Students see erasing as failure, but lifting out tone and correcting are standard observational drawing techniques.

items.correct: The lightness or darkness of areas, used to model three-dimensional form.
items.misconception: The colour used in the drawing.
items.why: Students confuse tone with colour; tone refers to values from light to dark, even in a monochrome drawing.

items.q: Which technique uses crossing lines for tone?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Cross-hatching. “Stippling” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Dots. “Lines” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many primary colours are commonly used in pigment mixing?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 colours. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
